• Show All Code
  • Hide All Code
  • Download Rmd

16 prep

library(tidyverse)

bad lm

Why is it weird to fit a linear model to binary outcome data?

mallow <- read_csv("https://uoepsy.github.io/data/mallow.csv")
mallow_lm <- lm(taken ~ age, data = mallow)

check assumptions of bad lm

car::crPlots(mallow_lm)

non-linear

tibble(residuals=mallow_lm$residuals) |> 
  ggplot(aes(x = residuals)) +
  geom_histogram()

plot(mallow_lm, which = 2)

not normal! pattern consistent with bounded outcome data.

plot(mallow_lm, which = 1)

again, consistent with only two possible outcome values.

try ot interpret coefs?

summary(mallow_lm)

Call:
lm(formula = taken ~ age, data = mallow)

Residuals:
     Min       1Q   Median       3Q      Max 
-0.84500 -0.32803 -0.00053  0.31739  0.86955 

Coefficients:
            Estimate Std. Error t value Pr(>|t|)    
(Intercept)  1.28890    0.14620   8.816 4.47e-14 ***
age         -0.12992    0.02269  -5.726 1.12e-07 ***
---
Signif. codes:  0 ‘***’ 0.001 ‘**’ 0.01 ‘*’ 0.05 ‘.’ 0.1 ‘ ’ 1

Residual standard error: 0.4371 on 98 degrees of freedom
Multiple R-squared:  0.2507,    Adjusted R-squared:  0.2431 
F-statistic: 32.79 on 1 and 98 DF,  p-value: 1.125e-07

if you either took a marshmallow = 1 or didn’t = 0, then what does it mean for the model to estimate that at age zero, the estimated outcome is 1.29? you SUPER took the marshmallow? weird.

look at model predictions

effects::effect(term = 'age', mod = mallow_lm, xlevels = list(age = 0:10)) |>
    as.data.frame() |>
    ggplot(aes(x = age, y = fit)) +
    geom_line() +
    geom_ribbon(aes(ymin = lower, ymax = upper), alpha = 0.3)

imposs values. (do this or coefs. i think this is better bc impossibility is clearer.)

plot logistic curve

copy code

p_logistic <- tibble(
  logodds = seq(-5, 5, length.out = 100),
  prob = plogis(logodds)
) |>
  ggplot(aes(x = logodds, y = prob)) +
  geom_line() +
  scale_x_continuous(expand = c(0, 0)) +
  scale_y_continuous(expand = c(0, 0), limits = c(0, 1)) +
  labs(
    y = 'Probability',
    x = 'Log-odds'
  ) +
  ggtitle('The logistic function')

p_logistic

transforming probs to log-odds

Based on the plot of the logistic curve, guess the approximate log-odds value that corresponds to each of these probabilities. Write down your guess.

probs <- c(0.33, 0.05, 0.99, 0.2, 0, 1)

Now compute the actual log-odds value for each probability and compare your guesses.

qlogis(probs)
[1] -0.7081851 -2.9444390  4.5951199 -1.3862944       -Inf        Inf

Try running the following lines of code. why does each one fail?

qlogis(-5)
[1] NaN
qlogis(1.5)
[1] NaN
qlogis(100)
[1] NaN

transforming log-odds to probs

guess

logodds <- c(-10, -2, 0.2, 5, 10)
plogis(logodds)
[1] 4.539787e-05 1.192029e-01 5.498340e-01 9.933071e-01 9.999546e-01

Can you find any values that make plogis() fail?

If you find some values, what values are they, and why does plogis() fail?

If there are no values, why does plogis() not fail?

plogis(-Inf)
[1] 0
plogis(Inf)
[1] 1

why back-transforming indiv model coefs into prob space does not work

Imagine a line in log-odds space. That line has an intercept of 1 and a slope of 0.5.

Here’s some data that corresponds to that line:

logodds_line_df <- tibble(
    predictor = 0:5,
    logodds_success = 1 + 0.5 * predictor
)

logodds_line_df

To plot the line:

logodds_line_df |>
    ggplot(aes(x = predictor, y = logodds_success)) +
    geom_line()

back-transform intercept to prob space.

plogis(1)
[1] 0.7310586

do the wrong thing and back-transform slope to prob space using plogis() as well, we get

plogis(0.5)
[1] 0.6224593

what value would this line predict for predictor = 1? predictor = 2?

0.73 + 0.62 * 2

[1] 1.97

so if that was a line in probability space, it would look like

tibble(
    predictor = 0:5,
    bad_prob_success = plogis(1) + plogis(0.5) * predictor
) |>
    ggplot(aes(x = predictor, y = bad_prob_success)) +
    geom_line()

how to back-transform this line to probabilities correctly:

we can only back-transform actual log-odds values, NOT differences between log-odds values.

and if you plot it, notice that it’s not a straight line, so it doesn’t make sense to have a constant slope in probability space!

plogis(1 + 0.5*0)
[1] 0.7310586
plogis(1 + 0.5*1)
[1] 0.8175745
plogis(1 + 0.5*2)
[1] 0.8807971
